answers: Treat this as a security impacting, cross team incident that requires 2nd level (Identity / Security / Network / Endpoint) response and IR coordination. The pattern best explained by the supplied records is: (A) credential harvesting PDFs that exfiltrate credentials to algina[.]co; (B) emergency sign in / CA / firewall changes applied to contain the phishing that then blocked non MFA interactive users and broke legacy/non interactive service logins; (C) a perimeter ACL/NAT or firewall change that made the partner's uploads time out even though internal hosts could still reach the file server; and (D) Teams camera losses that match per device OS/permission problems rather than a hardware failure or a single endpoint driver push. This combination needs 2nd level identity + security + network + endpoint teams to coordinate immediately [Data: Sources (4); Sources (11); Sources (34); Sources (42)]. Why I think this (evidence summary) - The phishing ticket documents two malicious PDFs that redirected to a fake Microsoft login and exfiltrated credentials to algina[.]co - the portal recorded the PDFs and containment notes [Data: Sources (4)]. - Incident records show an ADFS/client registration misconfiguration (EnableJWT = true and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders = true) previously caused non browser integrations to fail; similar token/ADFS behavior is a credible mechanism for breaking automated integrations that cannot complete delegated MFA or accept JWT tokens [Data: Sources (11); Reports (2, 28)]. - The incident corpus contains examples where teams temporarily excluded service accounts from MFA during connector reinstall and where emergency CA/geolocation/firewall proposals explicitly listed service-account exceptions (POS/backup accounts were explicitly named), indicating such operational exceptions and CA tightening have been used and caused outage risk [Data: Sources (35); Sources (4)]. - File transfer troubleshooting notes and a FileZilla ticket show WinSCP/SFTP tests to 10.10.10.10:21 and a later firewall policy removal / decommission of external FTP that would explain partner timeouts while internal reachability remained [Data: Sources (34); Reports (32)]. - Multiple Teams camera tickets were resolved by restoring Windows app privacy permissions (Device Manager still showed hardware); the ticket set contains no evidence of a tenant wide Intune/GPO camera push, so per device permission/OS state is most likely for the camera symptom set [Data: Sources (42); Reports (5)]. Immediate priority actions (what to do in the next 1-3 hours) 1. Treat this as an active security incident and escalate to IR / Identity Architecture / Network owners - do not leave containment/uncoordinated CA changes in place [Data: Sources (4)]. 2. Block the exfiltration domain (algina[.]co) at DNS/proxy/firewall and preserve logs (DNS, proxy, firewall, webserver) for forensics. Preserve the malicious PDF artifacts from the portal (don't alter them) [Data: Sources (4)]. 3. Identify likely clicked PDFs / impacted users and immediately force those users to: reset passwords, revoke refresh tokens (sign out everywhere) and re enroll MFA. Time box mitigations and note which accounts were excluded from emergency rules [Data: Sources (4); Sources (35)]. 4. Temporarily revert or scope back any tenant wide emergency CA/geolocation rules if they are denying business critical non interactive flows - but only after logs are exported/preserved. Instead create narrowly scoped exceptions for known service account principals while preserving evidence [Data: Sources (4); Sources (35)]. 5. Check ADFS/client registration flags (EnableJWT, RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) for any recently changed clients used by your automation (Toolbox/Train example) and revert those client registration fields back to the known good TEST values if they were changed in PROD [Data: Sources (11); Reports (2, 28)]. 6. For partner upload timeouts: do not assume partner change. Immediately collect partner timestamps/IPs and compare to your firewall/NAT logs and FileZilla server logs; if a perimeter ACL or NAT removal was applied (example: external FTP decommissioning), restore a monitored, temporary allowlist for partner IPs if business critical [Data: Sources (34); Reports (32)]. 7. Triage Teams camera cases by checking device privacy/app permissions, Intune assignment history, recent GPO pushes and EDR/Defender logs on the affected endpoints; if per device fixes resolve the issue, do not assume a tenant wide policy push unless audit records prove it [Data: Sources (42); Reports (5)]. Logs and artifacts to collect immediately (preserve before change) - Azure AD / Entra sign in logs for impacted users and for the named service accounts (filter the interval: since "yesterday afternoon" through now). Include timestamp, userPrincipalName, ipAddress, clientAppUsed, conditionalAccessPolicies, errorCode/status and correlationId [Data: Sources (31, 38)]. - Azure AD Audit logs (Conditional Access policy updates, Security Defaults toggles) - export CA policy change history (who changed what and when) and preserve it before you make more changes [Data: Sources (38)]. - AD FS server logs / token issuance traces (if you federate) for client IDs used by integrations; capture token type issued (JWT vs SAML) and MFA delegation events (RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) [Data: Sources (11)]. - Firewall / NAT / WAF config change audit and traffic logs (edge) - look for rule add/remove events and NAT translations around partner upload times and around the emergency change window [Data: Sources (15); Sources (34)]. - File transfer server logs (FileZilla / SFTP) and partner upload timestamps, source IPs, client logs (WinSCP/FileZilla) and traceroutes - collect both server and partner artifacts and preserve them [Data: Sources (34)]. - Endpoint browser history / EDR telemetry / process creations on workstations that opened the PDFs; collect Teams client logs for camera issues and Intune device configuration history for those devices [Data: Sources (4); Sources (42)]. What to ask the partner (exact minimal checklist) - Provide traceroute(s) to the FTP host (3 runs) with exact UTC timestamps; provide the public source IP(s) used and indicate if a VPN/proxy was used. Provide at least one successful and two failed attempt logs (client log or capture). Example triage checklist and reasons are in the FileZilla support notes [Data: Sources (34)]. How the symptoms map to likely root causes (concise) - PDF -> fake Microsoft login -> credential capture explains the phishing vector and justifies forcing resets and MFA. The portal recorded the PDFs and algina[.]co as the exfiltration target [Data: Sources (4)]. - Emergency CA / firewall tighten (or Security Defaults toggle) can prevent sign ins for non MFA users and will block automation that cannot do MFA; that pattern matches recorded emergency mitigations and past NDES/service account exceptions [Data: Sources (4); Sources (35)]. - ADFS/client registration toggles (EnableJWT + RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) will change token type and delegate MFA to claims providers, breaking non browser/service integrations - this exact cause was recorded in a Train/Toolbox incident in the ticket set [Data: Sources (11); Reports (2, 28)]. - Partner uploads timing out while internal reachability works is consistent with a perimeter NAT/ACL removal or firewall rule change (external FTP decommissioning is recorded in a past file transfer ticket) [Data: Sources (34); Reports (32)]. - Camera missing in Teams but visible in Device Manager correlates to per device app/OS privacy toggles or Intune/GPO settings rather than hardware failure; the tickets show per device permission fixes resolved similar cases [Data: Sources (42); Reports (5)]. Who/which teams must be involved now - Incident Response / Security / SOC (for credential compromise, domain blocking, forensic timeline). - Identity / Entra / AD FS team (for sign in logs, ADFS client registration checks and CA rollback/targeted exceptions). - Network / Perimeter / Firewall / WAF owners (for partner upload correlation, NAT and ACL reviews). - Application owners (File transfer server / POS / backup) to validate and restart scheduled jobs after credentials are rotated. - Endpoint / Intune / Teams owners (to triage camera/permission issues) [Data: Sources (4); Sources (11); Sources (34); Sources (42)]. Ticket classification: 1st level or 2nd level? - Classify this as 2nd level (Identity / Security / Network / Endpoint cross team incident / Incident Response). Rationale: suspected credential compromise (phishing) + tenant level sign in/Conditional Access changes + broken non interactive integrations + external partner connectivity are cross domain and require privileged audits, CA edits, AD FS client checks and firewall/NAT coordination - these are beyond routine 1st line fixes and require IR/Identity architecture involvement [Data: Sources (4); Sources (11); Sources (34); Reports (19)]. Concrete next steps I can help produce right now - If you want, I will produce exact copy paste Graph / PowerShell queries to export Azure AD Sign ins and Audit logs for the timeframe you specify (I cannot run them for you) - give me the start time (yesterday afternoon) in UTC and the list of UPNs or service account names to target. I can also draft the request text to send to your partner for traceroutes/client logs and a ready to run checklist for firewall/edge teams to extract NAT/firewall logs [Data: Sources (31, 34)]. If you want me to draft the sign in query and the partner email now, tell me: - The UTC timeframe you want (or say "yesterday afternoon -> now"), and - The UPNs / service account names you want prioritized (if you do not have them, I will use the service accounts found in the ticket notes as candidates). If you want any of the specific remediation commands (reverting the ADFS client flags, the exact Azure AD Graph query or PowerShell to export sign in logs, or the exact firewall log filters), say which one and I'll deliver them immediately.
